# VARefinance.com

> VARefinance.com is an independent educational resource about VA loan refinancing for veterans, active-duty service members, and military families. The site does not originate loans. It explains how VA refinance programs work, who qualifies, and how to evaluate whether refinancing makes sense.

The two primary VA refinance programs covered are the VA IRRRL (Interest Rate Reduction Refinance Loan, also called the VA Streamline Refinance) and the VA Cash-Out Refinance. The site also covers VA home purchase loans. All content is written for a veteran audience in plain, non-technical language with no sales pressure.

## Service Pages

- [VA Streamline Refinance (IRRRL)](https://www.varefinance.com/va-irrrl): Comprehensive guide to the VA IRRRL program — eligibility, benefits, process, costs, and FAQs. The IRRRL lets veterans with an existing VA loan refinance to a lower rate with minimal paperwork and no appraisal.
- [VA Cash-Out Refinance](https://www.varefinance.com/va-cash-out): Complete guide to the VA Cash-Out Refinance — how it works, equity limits, funding fees, Type I vs Type II, and who qualifies. Open to all eligible veterans, not just current VA loan holders.
- [VA Home Purchase](https://www.varefinance.com/va-purchase): Overview of using a VA loan to purchase a home — zero down payment, no PMI, eligibility requirements.
- [VA Refinance Decision Tool](https://www.varefinance.com/calculator): Interactive calculator that helps veterans compare IRRRL vs Cash-Out refinancing, estimates savings, checks net tangible benefit requirements, and calculates funding fees.

## Data & Tools

- [VA Refinance Market Snapshot — March 2026](https://www.varefinance.com/data/va-refinance-snapshot): Monthly VA refinance market snapshot — refinance-eligible population, savings scenarios, and refinance activity trends sourced from ICE Mortgage Monitor data.

## Blog Articles

- [What Is a VA IRRRL and How Can It Save You Money?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/what-is-va-irrrl): Explains how the VA Interest Rate Reduction Refinance Loan works, who qualifies, and realistic savings estimates.
- [VA Cash-Out Refinance: Tap Into Your Home Equity](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-cash-out-tap-into-equity): How the VA Cash-Out Refinance works, what you can use the funds for, and how it compares to the IRRRL.
- [5 Things Every Veteran Should Know About VA Loan Refinancing](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/5-things-veterans-should-know): Five critical facts every veteran homeowner should understand before refinancing or deciding not to.
- [How Long Do You Have to Wait to Refinance a VA Loan?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-loan-refinance-waiting-period): The 210-day rule, the 6-payment rule, and how waiting periods differ between IRRRL and Cash-Out.
- [VA IRRRL vs Conventional Refinance: Which Saves You More?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-irrrl-vs-conventional-refinance): Side-by-side comparison of VA IRRRL and conventional refinance — appraisal, documentation, closing costs, and long-term savings.
- [Type I vs Type II VA Cash-Out Refinance Explained](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/type-1-vs-type-2-va-cash-out-refinance): Type I keeps your loan balance at or below the payoff amount. Type II increases it. Explains the difference and when each applies.
- [How to Spot Predatory VA Refinance Offers](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/how-to-spot-predatory-va-refinance-offers): Red flags of predatory VA loan solicitations and how veterans can protect themselves, based on VA and CFPB warnings.
- [What Drives VA Mortgage Rates Up and Down?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/what-drives-va-mortgage-rates): Explains the factors that move VA mortgage rates — the Fed, bond markets, inflation, jobs data, and how veterans can track rate trends.
- [VA IRRRL Closing Costs: What to Expect and How to Roll Them In](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-irrrl-closing-costs): Covers the 0.5% funding fee, lender fees, title fees, and the option to roll closing costs into the loan vs paying out of pocket.
- [VA Funding Fee 2026: Complete Breakdown by Loan Type](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-funding-fee-2026): Current funding fee rates by loan type, service category, and down payment. Includes exemptions for disability, Purple Heart, and surviving spouses.
- [Refinancing a Conventional Loan Into a VA Loan: Step by Step](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/refinance-conventional-to-va-loan): How to refinance from a conventional mortgage into a VA loan using a Cash-Out refi — benefits include dropping PMI and potentially getting a better rate.
- [Can I Do a VA Streamline Refinance on a Rental Property?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-irrrl-rental-property): Explains occupancy rules for the VA IRRRL — veterans who previously lived in the home as a primary residence can refinance even after moving out.
- [How Much Equity Can I Cash Out With a VA Loan?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-cash-out-equity-limits): VA allows 100% LTV but most lenders cap at 90%. Compares VA, conventional, and FHA cash-out limits with dollar examples.
- [VA IRRRL Net Tangible Benefit: What It Means and Why It Matters](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-irrrl-net-tangible-benefit): The net tangible benefit rule requires a measurable financial improvement — at least 0.5% rate reduction for fixed-to-fixed, plus 36-month recoupment of costs.
- [Can I Reuse My VA Loan Benefit? Entitlement Restoration Explained](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-loan-entitlement-restoration): The VA loan benefit is a lifetime benefit that can be used unlimited times. Explains entitlement restoration, one-time restoration, and having two VA loans simultaneously.
- [VA Cash-Out Refinance vs HELOC: Which Is Better for Veterans?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-cash-out-vs-heloc): Side-by-side comparison of VA Cash-Out Refinance and HELOC — rates, costs, flexibility, and which is better for different scenarios. The VA does not offer a HELOC product.
- [VA IRRRL Documentation: What You Actually Need (and Don't Need)](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-irrrl-documents-needed): Most veterans need almost no paperwork for an IRRRL — no income docs, no appraisal, no new COE. Explains what lenders may add as overlays.
- [Can I Use a VA Cash-Out Refinance to Pay Off Debt?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-cash-out-refinance-debt-consolidation): Covers using VA Cash-Out Refinance proceeds for debt consolidation — credit card rates vs. mortgage rates, full break-even analysis including the impact on your existing mortgage rate, and warnings about converting unsecured debt into a secured mortgage obligation.
- [Can I Refinance a VA Loan Into a Conventional Loan?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/refinance-va-loan-to-conventional): When and why veterans refinance out of a VA loan into a conventional mortgage — rental property conversion, co-borrower removal, freeing up VA entitlement — and the real trade-offs including PMI, higher rates, and how VA benefit eligibility is affected.
- [How Many Times Can You Use the VA IRRRL?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/how-many-times-va-irrrl): No cap on IRRRL usage, but every refinance must meet the 210-day seasoning requirement and net tangible benefit test. Covers multi-IRRRL scenarios in a declining rate environment, compounding funding fees, and churning warnings.
- [VA IRRRL With a Second Mortgage: What You Need to Know](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-irrrl-second-mortgage-subordination): Explains subordination requirements when refinancing a VA loan with a HELOC or home equity loan — what subordination is, how to request it, timeline implications, what happens if the second lien holder refuses, and alternatives including payoff or switching to a VA Cash-Out Refinance.
- [ARM to Fixed Rate: How the VA Streamline Makes Switching Easy](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-irrrl-arm-to-fixed-rate): How veterans with adjustable-rate VA loans can convert to a fixed rate using the IRRRL. Covers the more lenient NTB rules for ARM-to-fixed conversions (new rate doesn't need to be lower), the recoupment rule when payment increases, and a concrete scenario of a 5/1 ARM approaching adjustment.
- [VA Cash-Out Refinance on a Paid-Off Home: How It Works](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-cash-out-refinance-paid-off-home): Veterans with free-and-clear homes can use a VA Cash-Out Refinance to access their equity — the full loan amount becomes available cash minus closing costs and funding fee. Covers dollar math at 90% LTV, full underwriting requirements, and the risk of introducing a new mortgage on a debt-free home.
- [VA Cash-Out Refinance Credit Score Requirements by Lender](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-cash-out-refinance-credit-score): The VA has no minimum credit score but lenders set overlays typically in the 620–680 range for cash-out refinances. Explains how score affects rate pricing, approval vs. getting a good deal, manual underwriting limits, and score improvement strategies.
- [How to Get Your VA Certificate of Eligibility (COE) Fast](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-certificate-of-eligibility-how-to-get): Three methods to obtain a COE — lender electronic pull via VA's WebLGY system (fastest, often minutes), VA.gov self-service, and mail via Form 26-1880 (4–6 weeks). Covers required documents by service type including DD-214, statement of service, and NGB Form 22, plus common issues like lost discharge papers, prior VA loan usage, and IRRRL as a special case where the lender typically handles the COE without borrower action.
- [VA Loan Benefits After Separation: What Veterans Need to Know](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-loan-benefits-after-separation): The VA loan benefit does not expire after military separation — veterans can use it decades after leaving service. Covers minimum service requirements by era (wartime, peacetime, post-1980 24-month rule, National Guard/Reserve), discharge status thresholds including OTH case-by-case review, entitlement reuse, the DD-214 and COE process, and four common post-separation scenarios.
- [Is Now a Good Time to Refinance Your VA Loan? How to Decide](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/when-to-refinance-va-loan): A decision framework built on three criteria: whether the rate reduction meets the VA's 0.5% net tangible benefit threshold, whether closing costs can be recouped within the borrower's likely time horizon (calculated as total costs ÷ monthly savings), and how long the veteran plans to stay in the home. Covers rate context for veterans who locked in at 7%+ vs. 3–4% during 2020–2021, when not to refinance, and the market-timing question.
- [VA Refinance Document Checklist: Everything You Need Before You Apply](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-refinance-document-checklist): Side-by-side comparison of documents required for a VA IRRRL vs. a VA Cash-Out Refinance — explaining why the IRRRL requires almost nothing (current mortgage statement, prior occupancy certification) while a cash-out requires full documentation (W-2s, tax returns, pay stubs, bank statements, COE, DD-214, homeowners insurance). Covers lender overlays, pre-application tips, and the COE WebLGY distinction.
- [What Happens to My Escrow When I Refinance My VA Loan?](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-refinance-escrow-refund): When a VA loan is refinanced, the old escrow account is closed and refunded after payoff — federal law requires return of funds within 20 business days of account closure. A new escrow account is established at closing, requiring an upfront deposit. Explains the timing gap between funding the new account and receiving the old refund, how to estimate refund amounts, the effect of recent tax disbursements on balance, and that this applies to both IRRRL and cash-out refinances.
- [Can My Spouse Use My VA Loan Benefit? Eligibility Rules Explained](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/spouse-va-loan-eligibility): Surviving spouse eligibility (including the 2019 Blue Water Navy Act restoration for surviving spouses who remarried after age 57), co-borrower rules and how a non-veteran co-borrower affects the VA guaranty and partial entitlement, spouse on title vs. on the loan, what happens to VA loan entitlement in a divorce, and whether surviving spouses can refinance an existing VA loan using the IRRRL.
- [VA Loan Assumption: What It Means for Buyers and Sellers in 2026](https://www.varefinance.com/blog/va-loan-assumption-2026): VA loans are assumable by anyone — the buyer does not need to be a veteran. Covers the qualification process through the current servicer, why a non-veteran assumption leaves the selling veteran's entitlement tied to the loan, entitlement substitution when an eligible veteran assumes the loan, the critical importance of requesting a release of liability, the 0.5% assumption fee, the equity gap problem, and why veterans who locked in at 3–4% in 2020–2021 hold especially valuable assumable loans in the current rate environment.

## About

- [About VARefinance.com](https://www.varefinance.com/about): Background on the site's mission, editorial approach, and commitment to veteran financial education.
